fact: January 1 falls exactly 10 days after the winter solstice, which means days are already getting longer in the Northern Hemisphere - but you can barely notice it yet!
In Antarctica, January 1 is smack in the middle of summer, and the sun never sets - penguins are having 24-hour daylight parties!
Why do so many animals seem extra sleepy around January 1? Because in cold places, this is peak hibernation season - bears, groundhogs, and bats are in their deepest winter snooze.
January 1 sits right in the middle of Capricorn season, making it one of the most "earthy" and stubborn days of the zodiac calendar.
Tree sap moves slower than molasses on January 1 in northern forests - it's so cold that maple trees are basically frozen solid from root to tip.
In the Northern Hemisphere, January 1 sunrises have this weird orange-pink glow because the sun stays low in the sky and light has to travel through more atmosphere.
Ocean temperatures in the Northern Hemisphere hit some of their coldest points around January 1, making sea ice thick enough in some places to walk on.
January 1 falls during the Quadrantids meteor shower peak, so you might see shooting stars if you look up at the right time and place.
In Australia and South America, January 1 is peak flower blooming season - while kids in North America are bundled up, kids down south are surrounded by colorful wildflowers.
Snow crystals formed around January 1 in super cold places can be perfectly hexagonal because the extreme cold makes ice molecules line up in their most organized patterns.
January 1 marks roughly the halfway point of winter in the Northern Hemisphere - you've survived half the cold, but you still have half to go!
